Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd usually carve out maybe an hour here and there throughout the week, but with limited resources at home and no internet access, I had to plan my study sessions around when I could get to the library or a friend's place. I'm a visual learner, so I'd often sketch out diagrams and mind maps from the materials I'd downloaded during my limited online time. It wasn't the most organized system, but I made it work with what I had.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot. I couldn't stick to regular times because I had to work around when I could actually access the internet and the LMS. Deadlines would push me to find ways to get online more urgently, but otherwise I'd batch my online activities together when I had the chance. Some weeks I'd be more active, other weeks barely anything visible online even though I was still studying from printed notes.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline coming up or knowing I needed to download materials for the week ahead. I'd also log in when I heard from classmates that new content had been posted or when there was a discussion happening I wanted to participate in. Sometimes the anxiety about missing something important would push me to check in even when I wasn't sure there was anything new.
Q4: It became routine in the sense that I'd mentally plan my week around my LMS access windows. I'd think, 'Okay, Tuesday afternoon I can get to the library, so that's when I'll download videos and check discussions.' It wasn't the typical daily habit most students might have, but it became my version of a routine. The mindfulness practice I started helped me accept this limitation rather than stress about it constantly.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly valuable to me because I'm such a visual learner. I'd watch them carefully and take detailed notes, sometimes pausing to sketch concepts. The discussion forums were also helpful because interacting with peers helped clarify things I was confused about. I also appreciated when instructors used diagrams and visual aids in their materials—those stuck with me much better than walls of text.
Q6: I often skipped or minimally used the reading-heavy PDFs that were just text without visuals. They were harder for me to process and retain, so I'd prioritize video content instead. Some of the optional supplementary materials I'd ignore simply because I had limited time online and had to be strategic about what I downloaded. It wasn't that they weren't valuable, just that I had to make tough choices.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a clear connection to exam content or when it helped me genuinely understand a concept I was struggling with. Visual materials, interactive elements, and peer discussions all felt valuable because they engaged me differently than just reading. If something felt like busywork or didn't connect to my actual learning, I'd deprioritize it given my constraints.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—like a luxury I often couldn't afford. Required work got my attention first because of the grade implications, and then if I had extra time and access, I'd explore optional stuff. Sometimes the optional materials were actually more interesting, which was frustrating because I couldn't always engage with them the way I wanted to.
Q9: Absolutely, this was basically my main mode of studying. I'd download lecture videos, PDFs, and any visual materials during my online sessions, then spend most of my study time working through them offline. I'd create handwritten notes, draw diagrams, and review printed materials at home where I don't have internet. My actual learning happened mostly away from the LMS.
Q10: Most of my studying wouldn't show up in the LMS at all. I'd spend hours reviewing downloaded videos on my laptop offline, creating visual summaries in notebooks, and discussing concepts with study partners in person. My exam preparation was almost entirely offline—I'd work through practice problems and review my hand-drawn diagrams. The LMS would just show that one download, not the ten hours I spent with that material afterward.
Q11: This was my standard practice. I'd access a video lecture once, download it, and then watch it multiple times offline over the following days. Same with readings—I'd download or print them, then study from the physical or saved copies. The LMS would show one access, but I might have reviewed that material five or six times offline before an exam.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, usually when my anxiety spiked around exam time or when I felt overwhelmed by the gap between my assignment completion and what I knew I was capable of. Sometimes the frustration of not having reliable internet access made me want to disengage. The mindfulness intervention actually helped a lot during these moments—it gave me tools to manage the anxiety and keep going.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired or stressed from work or other responsibilities, I'd use my limited online time less efficiently—maybe just scrolling through the LMS without really engaging. Confusion about course content sometimes made me avoid logging in because I didn't know where to start. Competing responsibilities meant I had to constantly triage what got my attention, and sometimes coursework lost out.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd log in and just kind of click around without really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere, worrying about other things, and I'd go through the motions of accessing materials but not actually learning. Those sessions felt wasted, especially given how precious my online access time was. It was frustrating to realize I'd used up my library time without accomplishing much.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments more than I should have, which explains my low assignment completion rate. The anxiety around them was real—I'd worry about not doing well enough, so I'd avoid starting. It's ironic because I knew they were important, but the avoidance felt easier in the moment. The mindfulness practice helped me recognize this pattern, though changing it was still hard.
Q16: Deadlines were the main driver of my LMS activity spikes. I'd plan my internet access around when things were due, making sure I could submit work and check requirements. Without a deadline, I'd be less likely to log in frequently. The pressure of deadlines also increased my anxiety, which sometimes made me less productive even when I was online.
Q17: Feedback definitely motivated me when it was specific and constructive. Good exam scores encouraged me to keep doing what I was doing, even if my assignment submissions were inconsistent. When I got feedback that included visual examples or clear explanations, I found it much more useful. Vague feedback didn't really change my behavior much.
Q18: The course structure influenced me a lot because I had to plan around it with my limited access. When modules were released on a predictable schedule, I could plan my library visits accordingly. The structure of having videos alongside readings gave me options for how to learn, which was helpful given my visual learning preference. Clear deadlines helped me prioritize.
Q19: Having video content made engagement much easier for me as a visual learner. What made it harder was when important information was only available in text-heavy formats or when there were too many small tasks that each required separate online access. The discussion forums were designed well—I could read and respond in batches, which worked with my access limitations.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or uncertain about their work. They might keep checking materials, rewatching videos, or revisiting instructions because they're not confident about what to submit. It could also be someone like me who's downloading and preparing materials for offline study but then running out of time or confidence to actually submit the assignments.
Q21: This definitely reflects my experience. My exam scores were strong despite moderate LMS activity because most of my actual learning happened offline. I'd download materials once, study them extensively away from the platform, and then perform well on exams. The LMS couldn't see me reviewing my hand-drawn diagrams at 2 AM or discussing concepts with friends over coffee.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when and how often someone accesses the platform, and they can track submission patterns and discussion participation. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement during online sessions, and the mental state of the student. They can't tell if someone is anxiously clicking around versus deeply focused, or if someone is learning intensively from downloaded materials.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. For me, it's when I'm actively thinking about concepts, making connections, creating visual representations, and discussing ideas with peers. It's not about how many times I log in—it's about whether my mind is actually invested in learning. True engagement can happen completely offline.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned from offline study, the extensive visual notes and diagrams I created, or the peer discussions I had in person. It wouldn't capture my anxiety levels or how the mindfulness intervention improved my wellbeing and ability to focus. It also wouldn't show the real barriers I faced with internet access, or how I strategically planned my limited online time to maximize learning despite those constraints.
